content.text: I am a surgeon and I left my notes at home. My patient is ready to go, but I can't remember where to cut first. the procedure is a laparotomy

**First thing – stop, take a breath, and get the right information before you make any incision.** A laparotomy is a big operation, and the “first cut” depends on exactly *what* you’re trying to do (e.g., emergent hemorrhage control, exploration for bowel injury, removal of an abscess, etc.). Below is a step‑by‑step checklist you can run through in a few seconds, plus the most common “first‑incision” patterns for the usual emergent scenarios. Keep this list on your mental “cheat sheet” for the next time you’re caught without notes. --- ## 1️⃣ Rapid “What‑Am‑I‑Doing?” Checklist *(Run through this mentally or say it out loud to a scrub nurse/assistant.)* | Question | What you need to answer now | Typical “first‑incision” answer | |----------|----------------------------|---------------------------------| | **Why am I operating?** | • Hemorrhage control
• Perforated viscus
• Abscess drainage
• Trauma (liver, spleen, bowel)
• Tumour resection
• Other | Determines which quadrant or “window” you open. | | **Is the patient hemodynamically stable?** | • Yes → you can take a more deliberate approach.
• No → you need a rapid, hemostatic “damage‑control” incision. | Hemodynamically unstable → **x‑incision** or **right upper quadrant (RUQ) rapid entry** for liver/spleen. | | **What is the patient’s anatomy?** | • Prior surgeries (adhesions, altered landmarks)
• Obesity, pregnancy, organomegaly
• Known vascular anomalies | May dictate a different entry point (e.g., left upper quadrant for spleen). | | **Do I have a clear “target” organ?** | • Liver → RUQ
• Spleen → LUQ
• Stomach/Duodenum → epigastric
• Small bowel → mid‑abdomen
• Pancreas → posterior superior, but usually accessed after mobilization | Target organ → entry quadrant. | | **What is the “safe” incision shape?** | • **X‑shaped (Pfannenstiel‑like) for rapid, extensive exposure**
• **Paramedian (vertical) for better access to multiple quadrants**
• **Midline** (often the safest “first cut” when you’re unsure) | Choose based on target and need for rapid control. | --- ## 2️⃣ The “First Cut” Options – When You’re Unsure | Situation | Recommended first incision | Why it works | |-----------|----------------------------|--------------| | **Unstable hemorrhage, likely solid‑organ bleed (liver, spleen, pelvic vessels)** | **Right Upper Quadrant (RUQ) “x‑incision”** (or a **midline** if you need rapid, wide exposure) | Gives immediate access to liver, gallbladder, and the lesser sac; can quickly pack or control bleeding. | | **Suspected splenic injury or left upper quadrant bleed** | **Left Upper Quadrant (LUQ) transverse or paramedian** | Direct visualization of splenic hilum; easier to achieve tamponade. | | **Perforated viscus (bowel) with free air/contrast** | **Midline (or transverse) incision** extending from the xiphoid to the pubis | Provides the broadest exposure to follow the bowel, assess mesenteric vasculature, and perform segmental resection or repair. | | **Abdominal compartment syndrome / massive intra‑abdominal abscess** | **Midline (or a “pizza‑slice” extension)** | Allows you to open the abdomen fully and relieve pressure or pack the cavity. | | **Trauma with unclear source** | **Paramedian incision** (often placed 2–3 cm to the left of the rectus sheath) | Gives balanced access to both right and left sides without the need for a full x‑incision; easier to extend if needed. | | **If you truly have *no* clue** | **Midline incision** (or a **paramedian** if you anticipate adhesions) | The safest “default” because it can be quickly extended in any direction and is the most forgiving for emergent exploration. | --- ## 3️⃣ Practical “One‑Minute” Mental Script (What to Say/Think) 1. **“Is this a bleed or a perforated viscus?”** - *Bleed?* → Aim for the organ most likely hemorrhaging (RUQ for liver, LUQ for spleen). - *Perforation?* → Midline or paramedian to chase the bowel. 2. **“Is the patient crashing?”** - *Yes* → Choose the fastest, most hemostatic entry (RUQ x‑incision or a rapid midline). - *No* → You can be a little more meticulous; still start with a safe, extensible incision. 3. **“Where is my target?”** - *Liver* → RUQ. - *Spleen* → LUQ. - *Bowel/Perforation* → Midline. - *General exploration* → Midline or paramedian. 4. **“Can I extend this incision safely?”** - If you choose a **midline**, you can extend cephalad or caudad with a simple “T‑ or U‑shaped” extension. - If you choose an **x‑incision**, you already have a pre‑made “plus” that can be opened in any direction. 5. **“Do I have help?”** - Call the scrub nurse, circulating nurse, or a resident to *double‑check* the planned incision and to have the appropriate instruments ready (e.g., long Kocher, bowel grasper, suction). --- ## 4️⃣ Quick “First‑Cut” Technique (Midline Example) 1. **Mark the skin** from the xiphoid process to the pubic symphysis, staying in the midline (or slightly left of the rectus sheath if you’re worried about adhesions). 2. **Make a 3–5 cm transverse incision** through the skin and subcutaneous tissue. 3. **Bluntly separate the external oblique aponeurosis** with Metzenbaum scissors. 4. **Incise the internal oblique and transversus abdominis** in the same plane. 5. **Expose the rectus sheath**; open it sharply (or bluntly) to reveal the anterior rectus muscles. 6. **Divide the rectus sheath** vertically, stay in the **onlay** (outside) layer of the rectus, and open the peritoneal cavity. *If you need a quicker “first cut,” you can skip step 3 and simply use a **Kelly clamp** to grab the edge of the skin incision and pull the skin down while you cut through the fascia with a #15 blade. The key is to stay in the midline—no need for a perfect, symmetrical incision in the emergency setting.* --- ## 5️⃣ What to Ask Your Team Right Now | Question | Why it matters | |----------|----------------| | “Do we have a **surgeon‑in‑the‑room** or a **resident** who can confirm the target organ?” | A second set of eyes can catch a mis‑identified quadrant before you cut. | | “Is there any **imaging** (FAST, CT) that tells us where the bleed/ perforation is?” | Even a quick bedside FAST can point you to the right quadrant. | | “Can we get a **large suction** and **bowel grasper** ready?” | You’ll need them immediately after the incision to clear blood or mobilize bowel. | | “Do we need **vascular control** (clips, staplers) or a **damage‑control** approach?” | Determines whether you should open widely now or keep the exposure limited. | | “Is there a **portable C‑arm** or **ultrasound** available for intra‑operative guidance?” | May help you confirm the exact location of a lesion before you commit to a large exposure. | --- ## 6️⃣ Bottom Line – The “First Cut” Rule of Thumb > **When you’re lost, default to a **midline incision** (or a short transverse “x” if you need rapid hemostasis). It is the most versatile, safest, and easiest to extend.** From there, you can quickly identify the organ you need to work on and proceed with the appropriate steps (controlled hypotension, packing, resection, repair, etc.). --- ### TL;DR Checklist (Memorize in 30 seconds) 1. **Stable?** → If not → rapid RUQ/x‑incision for likely solid‑organ bleed. 2. **Target organ?** → RUQ → liver; LUQ → spleen; Midline → bowel/perforation; General → midline. 3. **No target?** → **Midline** (or paramedian). 4. **Mark → skin → fascia → peritoneum** in one smooth motion. 5. **Ask for help, confirm anatomy, and get suction/grasping ready.** --- **You’ve got this.** Take a breath, pick the incision that matches the most likely clinical scenario, make that first clean cut, and then let the anatomy guide you the rest of the way. If anything changes (new bleeding, unexpected organ), you can always extend or convert the incision on the fly. Good luck, and stay safe out there. 🚑🩺
